original_abstract: Overcoming the challenge of metal contamination in traditional ATRP systems, a metal-free ATRP process, mediated by light and catalyzed by an organic-based photoredox catalyst, is reported. Polymerization of vinyl monomers are efficiently activated and deactivated with light leading to excellent control over the molecular weight, polydispersity, and chain ends of the resulting polymers. Significantly, block copolymer formation was facile and could be combined with other controlled radical processes leading to structural and synthetic versatility. We believe that these new organic-based photoredox catalysts will enable new applications for controlled radical polymerizations and also be of further value in both small molecule and polymer chemistry.
humanized_abstract: There have been longstanding issues with metal contamination when using traditional ATRP. We have developed a metal-free catalytic technique to achieve ATRP under photonic activation using an organic photoredox catalyst as the transfer agent. Under activation and deactivation by light, the vinyl monomers are efficiently polymerised and growth and termination of the polymer chains are strictly controlled, resulting in narrow polydispersities, well-defined molecular weights and precise chain ends. In addition, the synthesis of block copolymers is achievable, and can be further combined with other methods of controlled radical polymerisation to produce a range of advanced polymer architectures. We believe that the use of our organic photoredox catalysts in controlled radical polymerisation, as well as their application to small molecule transformations and polymer chemistry, will significantly open the scope of new materials that can be produced.
